author: @jarrellmoss4621
text: Not an attorney, but I have seen my fair share of court cases. When a defense takes on a case, they can do several motions. One of these motions is a motion of discovery. For example, if an officer makes a traffic stop for speeding and that officer used or claimed to have used a RADAR to  determine the driver's speed. A defense attorney files a motion of discovery, which gives the attorney multiple pieces of information. Is the Officer certified to use a RADAR, is the RADAR that was used properly? Was the RADAR certified at the time of use? So, in a much larger case, such as a murder trial, there shouldn't be an issue with a defense attorney or defense team being unable to have discovery on parts of the case.

author: @mythmurzin
text: by law, the prosecution must turn over all documents and copies of evidence to the defense prior to the trial, and with enough time to vet it.  especially if it is exculpatory evidence, failure to do so can get the case thrown out on appeal, possibly blocking re-trial under double jeopardy.  if you want him to get off on the assassination then you do not want the prosecution to turn copies of everything over, because that could be used to free him on appeal.  if you want him to go to jail for what he did and for a long time, then you want the prosecution to turn everything over, and enough time to vet it so it cannot be used on appeal.
